0.1, Ca2+ = 10.0</td></tr><tr><td>+ 120 YF3</td><td>[CaF2·YF3](ss); XCaF2(ss) = 0.69</td><td>600</td><td>78</td><td>53</td><td>Ce3+ = 0.1, Y3+ = 5.1, Ca2+ = 10.0</td></tr></table>

aNumbers indicate millimoles of reagent added per mole (63.2 g) of LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ (72-16-12 mole%).   
$b$ Phases in brackets are presumed to be present but were not identified.   
ND: not determined.

revealed an appreciable extraction of rare earths. Analysis of the salt phase also suggested the presence of $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{PO}_4^{3-}$ in solids, but these were not further identified (Table 13.2).

Reaction 9 was suggested by the phase diagram of the $\mathrm{CaF}_2\text{-YF}_3$ system, wherein a solid solution containing as much as 55 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{YF}_3$ as well as the compound $\mathrm{CaF}_2\cdot 4\mathrm{YF}_3$ is reported. The solubility of calcium at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in the presence and absence of $\mathrm{PO_4}^{3-}$ (8.8 and 7.8 mole $\%$ , respectively) and the percentage of cerium remaining in the melt (78 and 76) may be interpreted to mean that the solid phases present in both experiments were solid solutions of $\mathrm{CaF}_2\text{-YF}_3$ with some $\mathrm{CeF}_3$ . Under experimental conditions studied, the distribution of cerium seems insufficiently favorable and the solubility of the calcium-containing solids too high to render reactions 8 and 9 a promising basis for a separation method for rare earths.

While these tests have all given essentially negative results, they have produced some useful information. As expected, $\mathrm{TiO_2}$ and $\mathrm{Nb}_2\mathrm{O}_5$ , currently being studied for the first time, along with $\mathrm{Cr_2O_3}$ and $\mathrm{Fe_3O_4}$ ,studied previously, react only to a small extent with the molten fluoride. It appears that the orthophosphate ion is stable in such systems and does not act as an oxidant toward nickel containers. Finally, the solubility of $\mathrm{CaF_2}$ in the salt used (72-16-12 mole $\%$ LiF-BeF2- $\mathrm{ThF_4})$ is evidently 10 mole $\%$ at $600^{\circ}C.$

# 13.2 THE OXIDE CHEMISTRY OF PLUTONIUM IN MOLTEN FLUORIDES

C. E. Bamberger

R.G.Ross

C.F.Baes, Jr.

Since plutonium is being considered as a fuel for molten-salt reactors, it is important to examine its chemistry in molten fluoride systems containing oxide. Such information is needed to establish in a reactor fuel salt the conditions of redox potential and oxide concentration under which plutonium would remain in solution as $\mathrm{PuF_3}$ . It might also be of value in developing reprocessing methods for such fuel salts.

From the available data2-5 it is possible to construct the Pourbaix diagram shown in Fig. 13.1, which summarizes the predicted behavior of plutonium in molten $\mathbf{Li}_2\mathbf{BeF}_4$ as a function of the oxidation potential (the ordinate) and the oxide ion concentration (the abscissa). In addition to the three oxides of plutonium $(\mathrm{PuO}_{1.5},\mathrm{PuO}_{1.61}$ ,and $\mathrm{PuO_2})$ whose stabilities have been determined,4 it is possible that PuOF may be

formed in the present system, but there is insufficient evidence to judge its stability. The boundaries of the region of dissolved $\mathrm{PuF_3}$ in this diagram are calculated for two concentrations; one is the hypothetical unit mole fraction and the other is 0.002 mole fraction, which is approximately the concentration of $\mathrm{PuF_3}$ which would be used in a molten-salt reactor fuel. These boundaries are uncertain by as much as plus or minus one log unit along each axis from the uncertainty given for the free energy of formation of $\mathrm{PuF_3}$ . Within such a wide uncertainty one cannot rule out the possibility that plutonium might precipitate as $\mathrm{PuO_{1.5}}$ from a molten-salt reactor fuel which becomes contaminated with sufficient oxide. At lower oxide concentrations, but under relatively oxidizing conditions, $\mathrm{PuO_2}$ clearly could be precipitated. With the $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ present in an MSBR fuel, the formation of a $\mathrm{PuO_2 - ThO_2}$ solid solution becomes possible, and this should lower the oxide level or the oxidation potential at which a plutonium oxide-containing phase might precipitate.

Because of the uncertainties in the behavior represented by Fig. 13.1 we were prompted to attempt a direct measurement of one of the equilibria which establish the boundaries of the dissolved $\mathbf{PuF}_3$ region in a diagram such as this. The result would not only improve the accuracy of such a diagram for plutonium; it should also yield an improved value of the formation free energy of $\mathbf{PuF}_3$ .

To various molten fluoride mixtures containing $\mathrm{PuF}_3$ , stepwise additions were made of an oxide $(\mathrm{BeO}, \mathrm{ZrO}_2$ , or $\mathrm{ThO}_2)$ known to be the normally stable saturating oxide phase. The equilibrations were performed in a specially designed leak-tight stirred vessel, described elsewhere, which had been assembled inside a glove box suited for work with alpha emitters. Filtered samples of the fluoride phase and unfiltered samples of both oxide and fluoride phases were taken for analysis.

![](images/fdcff90ad980c51a932c520e13ce12d97bbeb752467a401c1e778943c8ecf723.jpg)  
Fig. 13.1. Pourbaix Diagram for Plutonium in $\mathsf{Li}_2\mathsf{BeF}_4$ at $600^{\circ}\mathbf{C}$ Based on Literature Data (Refs. 1-3 of Sect. 13.1 and Refs. 1 and 9 of Sect. 13.2). Boundaries of the $\mathsf{PuF}_3$ and $\mathsf{PuF}_4$ regions indicate conditions of oxidation potential and oxide concentration under which these components, dissolved at the concentrations indicated, are at equilibrium with another phase. The heavy vertical line at the bottom indicates the upper limit of oxide concentration set by the solubility of BeO.

In the following molten salt mixtures, which contained up to 0.54 mole $\% \mathrm{PuF}_3$ , no detectable amount of dissolved plutonium was found to be precipitated by additions of the indicated stable oxide phases:

$$
\mathrm {L i F - B e F _ {2} - Z r F _ {4}} (6 5. 7 - 2 9. 3 - 5 \text {m o l e}
$$

$$
\mathrm {LiF - B e F} _ {2} (6 6. 7 - 3 3. 3 \text {mole}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {LiF - B e F _ {2}} (6 6. 7 - 3 3. 3 \text {mole} \\ \mathrm {LiF - B e F _ {2} - T h F _ {4}} (7 2 - 16 - 1 2 \text {m o l e} \\ \end{array}
$$

However, when NiO was added to the last salt in the presence of $\mathrm{ThO_2}$ , there was a decrease in the dissolved $\mathrm{PuF_3}$ concentration, presumably as a result of the reaction

Table 13.3 Equilibrium Data for the Reaction   
$\mathrm{PuF}_3(d) + \frac{3}{4}\mathrm{ThO}_2(ss) + \frac{1}{2}\mathrm{NiO}(c)\rightleftharpoons \mathrm{PuO}_2(ss) + \frac{3}{4}\mathrm{ThF}_4(d) + \frac{1}{2}\mathrm{Ni}^0 (c)$   

<table><tr><td>Temperature (°C)</td><td>XPuF3</td><td>XPuO2</td><td>Q1 = XPuO2(XThF4)/XPuF3(ThO2)3/4</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4">590</td><td>0.00210 ± 0.00011a</td><td>0.0894 ± 0.0028b</td><td>9.44 ± 0.84</td></tr><tr><td>0.00216 ± 0.00011a</td><td>0.0878 ± 0.0028b</td><td>9.00 ± 0.80</td></tr><tr><td>0.00253 ± 0.00015b</td><td>0.0781 ± 0.0039c</td><td>6.76 ± 0.72</td></tr><tr><td>0.00175 ± 0.00019b</td><td>0.0983 ± 0.0049d</td><td>12.58 ± 1.87</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4">690</td><td colspan="2">Weighted mean</td><td>8.51 ± 0.44</td></tr><tr><td>0.00469 ± 0.00024a</td><td>0.0219 ± 0.0061b</td><td>0.97 ± 0.34</td></tr><tr><td>0.00475 ± 0.00004b</td><td>0.0204 ± 0.0010d</td><td>0.89 ± 0.05</td></tr><tr><td colspan="2">Weighted mean</td><td>0.89 ± 0.05</td></tr></table>

$a$ From analysis of filtered fluoride phase.   
$b$ By material balance.   
cFrom lattice parameter measurements of the oxide phase by x-ray diffraction.   
$a_{\mathrm{From}}$ alpha pulse-height analysis of the oxide phase.

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {P u F} _ {3} (d) + 3 / 4 \mathrm {T h O} _ {2} (s s) + 1 / 2 \mathrm {N i O} (c) \rightleftharpoons \\ \mathrm {P u O} _ {2} (s s) + \frac {3}{4} \mathrm {T h F} _ {4} (d) + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {N i} (c) \tag {1} \\ \end{array}
$$

$(d,ss,$ and $c$ denote, respectively, the molten fluoride, solid solution, and pure crystalline phases). Samples of the equilibrated oxides, washed free of fluoride, gave $\mathbf{x}$ ray powder patterns characteristic of the $(\mathrm{Pu - Th})\mathrm{O}_2$ solid solution, confirming that the above reaction had occurred. Equilibrium was assumed to have been attained when the $\mathrm{PuF_3}$ content of filtered samples became constant with time within the analytical scatter of $\sim \pm 5\%$ .Final values of $X_{\mathrm{PuF_3}}$ along with values of $X_{\mathrm{PuO_2}}$ calculated by material balance are shown for two temperatures in Table 13.3. The table includes, as well, oxide samples whose composition was determined by alpha pulse-height analysis or from the lattice parameter determined by x-ray diffraction.

The equilibrium quotient of reaction (1),

$$
Q = \left(\frac {X _ {\mathrm {T h F} _ {4}}}{X _ {\mathrm {T h O} _ {2}}}\right) ^ {3 / 4} \frac {X _ {\mathrm {P u O} _ {2}}}{X _ {\mathrm {P u F} _ {3}}}, \tag {2}
$$

was calculated from these results and from values for $X_{\mathrm{ThF_4}}$ and $X_{\mathrm{ThO_2}}$ which were known from material balance. It can be seen in Table 13.3 that the largest analytical uncertainties occurred in the composition of the equilibrated $(\mathrm{Pu - Th})\mathrm{O}_2$ phase at $590^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and these are reflected in the corresponding uncertainties of $X_{\mathrm{PuF_3}}$ and $Q$ . The temperature has a marked effect on the value of the equilibrium quotient; between $590^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $690^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $Q$ changes by a factor of 10.

The resulting values of $Q$ for reaction (1) were used to estimate the free energy of formation of $\mathrm{PuF_3}$ in the $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{i}_2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{e}\mathbf{F}_4$ reference salt. The free energy of reaction (1) in this solvent is given by

$$
\Delta G _ {1} = - R T \ln \left(Q g _ {\mathrm {T h F} _ {4}} ^ {3 / 4} / g _ {\mathrm {P u F} _ {3}}\right), \tag {3}
$$

wherein activity coefficients in the solid solution are assumed to be unity and $g_{\mathrm{ThF_4}}$ and $g_{\mathrm{PuF_3}}$ are activity coefficients in the molten salt used for the measurement, $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ThF_4}$ (72-16-12 mole %). These are presently estimated as 0.40 and 0.35 respectively (Sect. 13.6). Combining $\Delta G_{1}$ with the following formation free energies:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Delta G _ {\mathrm {P u O} _ {2}} ^ {\circ} = - 2 5 2. 6 7 + 4 5. 9 0 (T / 1 0 0 0) \\ \pm 2 \mathrm {k c a l} / \text {m o l e} \quad (\text {r e f .} 4) \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Delta G _ {\mathrm {T h O} _ {2}} ^ {\circ} = - 2 9 2. 4 0 + 4 4. 5 5 (T / 1 0 0 0) \\ \pm 1 \mathrm {k c a l} / \text {m o l e} (\text {r e f .} 7) \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Delta G _ {\mathrm {N i O}} ^ {\circ} = - 5 6. 2 6 + 2 0. 3 5 (T / 1 0 0 0) \\ \pm 0. 2 \mathrm {k c a l} / \text {m o l e} \quad (\text {r e f .} 8) \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Delta \bar {G} _ {\mathrm {T h F} _ {4}} (\mathrm {L} _ {2} \mathrm {B}) = - 4 9 1. 1 9 + 6 2. 4 1 (T / 1 0 0 0) \\ \pm 2. 2 \mathrm {k c a l} / \text {m o l e} \quad (\text {r e f .} 5) \\ \end{array}
$$

we calculate the following formation free energies of $\mathbf{PuF}_3$ in $\mathbf{Li}_2\mathbf{BeF}_4$ in the hypothetical unit mole fraction standard state:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Delta \bar {G} _ {\mathrm {P u F} _ {3}} (\mathrm {L} _ {2} \mathrm {B}) = - 3 2 6. 9 6 \pm 2. 6 \mathrm {k c a l / m o l e} (5 9 0 ^ {\circ} \mathrm {C}) \\ = - 3 2 5. 6 7 \pm 2. 6 \mathrm {k c a l / m o l e} (6 9 0 ^ {\circ} \mathrm {C}) \\ \end{array}
$$

The assumption of ideality of the $\mathrm{PuO_2 - ThO_2}$ solid solution probably introduces a negligible uncertainty, as does any error in the estimates of activity coefficients in the salt phase.

Combining the above values of $\Delta \overline{G}_{\mathbf{PuF}_3}$ with the free energy of solution of $\mathbf{PuF}_3$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Delta \bar {G} _ {\mathrm {P u F} _ {3}} (\mathrm {L} _ {2} \mathrm {B}) - \Delta G _ {\mathrm {P u F} _ {3}} ^ {\circ} \\ = 1 4. 4 1 - 6. 0 5 (T / 1 0 0 0) \pm 0. 0 9 \\ \end{array}
$$

(based on Barton's solubility measurements), we obtain the following formation free energies of $\mathrm{PuF}_3(c)$ :

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Delta G _ {\mathrm {P u F} _ {3}} (c) = - 3 3 6. 1 1 \pm 2. 6 \mathrm {k c a l / m o l e} (5 9 0 ^ {\circ} \mathrm {C}) \\ = - 3 3 4. 4 6 \pm 2. 6 \mathrm {k c a l / m o l e} (6 9 0 ^ {\circ} \mathrm {C}) \\ \end{array}
$$

The values are 16 to 20 kcal more negative, in the temperature range studied, than values reported by Oetting in his review on thermodynamic properties of plutonium compounds. The values of Oetting are those calculated by Buyers and Murbach in 1957 from measurements of the equilibrium

$$
\frac {1}{n} \mathrm {U F} _ {n} (l) + \frac {1}{3} \mathrm {P u} (l) \rightleftharpoons \frac {1}{3} \mathrm {P u F} _ {3} (l) + \frac {1}{n} \mathrm {U} (l). \tag {4}
$$

Buyers and Murbach used the free energies of formation per fluoride of both $\mathrm{UF_4}$ and $\mathrm{UF_3}$ available at that date (88 and 89 kcal at $1573^{\circ}\mathrm{K}$ , respectively) and concluded that an accurate determination of $n$ was not necessary since it did not affect significantly the calculation of $\Delta G_{\mathrm{PuF}_3}$ . However, using more recent estimates of the corresponding free energies of formation of $\mathrm{UF_4}$ and $\mathrm{UF_3}$ (86.5 and 93.0 at $1573^{\circ}\mathrm{K}$ ), it is apparent that $\mathrm{UF_3}$ was the most stable fluoride present under the experimental conditions of Buyers and Murbach. This information together with their experimental data yields values of $\Delta G_{\mathrm{PuF}_3}$ which are consistent with the present estimates.

By combining our value of $\Delta G_{\mathbf{PuF_3}}$ with the equilibria

$$
4 \mathrm {P u F} _ {3} + \mathrm {O} _ {2} \xrightarrow [ \text {v a c u u m} ]{6 0 0 ^ {\circ} \mathrm {C}} 3 \mathrm {P u F} _ {4} + \mathrm {P u O} _ {2} \tag {5}
$$

studied by Dawson et al.10 we obtain the following free energy of formation of $\mathbf{PuF}_4(c)$ ..

$$
\Delta G _ {\mathrm {P u F} _ {4}} = - 3 8 7. 9 2 + 9. 9 5 (T / 1 0 0 0) \pm 2. 8 \mathrm {k c a l} / \mathrm {m o l e}.
$$

A revised Pourbaix diagram which incorporates these new formation free energies of $\mathbf{PuF}_3$ and $\mathbf{PuF}_4$ is shown in Fig. 13.2. The considerably more negative values of these free energies cause the boundaries between the dissolved fluorides and the solid oxides of plutonium to shift about three decades to the right, toward higher dissolved oxide concentrations. This result makes it abundantly clear why $\mathbf{PuO}_{1.5}$ could not be precipitated from $\mathbf{Li}_2\mathbf{BeF}_4$ saturated with BeO. Figure 13.3 shows the predicted behavior in the LiF- $\mathbf{BeF}_2$ -ThF4 (72-16-12 mole $\%$ ) melt, with various compositions shown for the $\mathbf{PuO}_2$ -ThO2 solid solution. It is apparent that under the mildly reducing conditions $(X_{\mathbf{UF}_4} / X_{\mathbf{UF}_3}\sim 100)$ of an MSBR fuel, even if an oxide phase were inadvertently precipitated it would contain no significant amount of $\mathbf{PuO}_2$

# 13.3 THE $\mathbf{CeF}_3$ -ThF4 SYSTEM

L. O. Gilpatrick H. Insley C. J. Barton

Exploration of the $\mathbf{CeF}_3$ -ThF $_4$ system was undertaken as part of the long-range investigation of the LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ -CeF $_3$ system. It should, however, have considerable scientific interest because, except for t.l.e system $\mathbf{UF}_3$ - $\mathbf{UF}_4$ , little is known about the binary systems of the heavy-metal tri- and tetrafluorides.

Our studies have been limited to gradient quenches because the high liquidus and solidus temperatures for the system exceed the capability of our present differential thermal analysis equipment. The end members were purified by fusion with ammonium bifluoride to remove oxides, and weighed quantities of the purified materials were blended to make mixtures containing 10 to 95 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ . The mixtures were sealed in the standard thin-wall nickel quench tubes and heated in a furnace having a platinum- $10\%$ rhodium heating element. The maximum temperature of these

![](images/7f46c28fa087a33f7be5c4b5a85f7cc5adb90f6af4d7a4c1803822ddbb3fe6c1.jpg)  
Fig. 13.2. Revised Pourbaix Diagram for Plutonium in $\mathbf{Li}_2\mathbf{BeF}_4$ at $600^{\circ}\mathbf{C}$ Based on Present Results. The boundaries of the $\mathbf{PuF_3}$ and $\mathbf{PuF_4}$ regions are shifted downward and to the right, reflecting changes of the free energies of formation for these compounds to more negative values.

quenches was about $1200^{\circ}\mathbf{C}$ , and the equilibration period varied from $1\frac{1}{2}$ to 3 days.

Rather limited data have been obtained because of difficulty experienced with self-welding between the nickel quench tubes and the nickel block that surrounds the tubes. The data obtained to date show that $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ is the primary phase in mixtures containing more than 81 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ . There appears to be a eutectic composition formed between $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ and a new phase, presumably a compound that is as yet unidentified,

melting at $975^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Another unidentified phase appears as the first crystals to separate from melts containing 67 and 70 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ . There is also evidence of solid solution of $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ in $\mathrm{CeF_3}$ , but the extent of solid solution formation remains to be determined.

We have modified our high-temperature quench furnace by introducing a thin graphite liner into the nickel quench block in an effort to overcome the self-welding problem. If this is successful, study of this system should be completed in the near future.

![](images/2df53eb5d470b826d66851c5c84039b2295dee78968ab0d1e2fdacc0eaf40c08.jpg)  
Fig. 13.3. Pourbaix Diagram for Plutonium in $\mathsf{LiF - BeF}_2\cdot \mathsf{ThF}_4$ (72-16-12 mole %) at $600^{\circ}\mathbf{C}$ Based on the Present Results. The upper limit of oxide concentration is set by the solubility of $\mathsf{ThO}_2$ . $\mathsf{PuO}_2\cdot \mathsf{ThO}_2$ solid solutions are formed at the higher oxidation potentials.

# 13.4 EQUILIBRIUM PHASE RELATIONSHIPS IN THE SYSTEM LiF-BeF $_2$ -CeF $_3$

L. O. Gilpatrick H. Insley C. J. Barton

Investigations of phase equilibria in the system $\mathbf{LiF - BeF}_2\mathbf{-CeF}_3$ have continued. Advances were made principally through use of the thermal gradient quench technique. This system is of interest in part because of its presumed analogy with the system LiF-BeF2

$\mathbf{PuF}_3,^{1,2}$ which has some interest as a possible fuel composition for molten-salt reactors. Compositions

were chosen to help define the lower-melting phase boundaries. This resulted in studying compositions containing less than 10 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{CeF_3}$ . The cerium fluoride primary phase field dominates the system, as might be expected from its high melting point of $1460^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and from the fact that LiF and $\mathrm{CeF_3}$ do not form intermediate compounds. This field approaches within 0.5 mole $\%$ of the low-melting LiF-BeF $_2$ binary composition ranging from 33 to approximately 60 mole $\%$ BeF $_2$ , where the freezing temperatures of LiF-BeF $_2$ compositions are less than $480^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . An earlier estimate that the maximum solubility of $\mathrm{CeF_3}$ and $\mathrm{PuF_3}$ in an LiF-BeF $_2$ melt containing 52 mole $\%$ BeF $_2$ is approximately 0.5 mole $\%$ at $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is therefore confirmed. The present findings are also consistent with the results of solubility measurements with $\mathrm{CeF_3}$ in LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ melts. $^5$

Because of the low solubility of $\mathrm{CeF}_3$ in the ternary system in this composition region, there is little difference between the minimum melting point of the LiF-BeF $_2$ binary system $(360^{\circ}\mathrm{C})^{6}$ and that of the LiF-BeF $_2$ -CeF $_3$ ternary, currently estimated to be $358^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The composition of this ternary invariant point is yet to be determined accurately, but it lies near the composition LiF-BeF $_2$ -CeF $_3$ (46.5-53-0.5 mole%).

Some evidence has been found for two immiscible liquids in equilibrium in a small region of this system which is rich in $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ and near the $\mathrm{CeF}_3$ - $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ eutectic composition, currently estimated to contain $6 \pm 1$ mole $\% \mathrm{CeF}_3$ . This eutectic has a melting point of $540 \pm 5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . An equilibration time longer than the one to three weeks currently used may be needed to attain equilibrium in this system, where equilibrium is attained slowly at those compositions high in $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ due to the high viscosity of the melts. Further work will be needed to substantiate these findings and to define the boundary of the region of immiscibility.

# 13.5 AN INVESTIGATION OF POSSIBLE POLYMORPHIC TRANSITIONS IN URANIUM TETRAFLUORIDE

L. O. Gilpatrick

Crystalline uranium tetrafluoride was reported by Khripin and co-workers $^{1,2}$ to undergo a dimorphic

crystal transition at a temperature of $841^{\circ}\mathbf{C}$ . Their observations were made by thermal analysis while studying the binary system $\mathrm{UF_4 - UF_3}$ . Since polymorphic crystal transitions have not been previously reported in this widely studied compound this report aroused some interest. Attempts were made to confirm this observation using differential thermal analysis apparatus which we developed recently for molten-salt studies.

A sample of depleted $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_4$ prepared at the Oak Ridge Gaseous Diffusion Plant and designated SR-1909, batch 4, was examined first, in the temperature range 700 to $1010^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with a differential temperature sensitivity of 50 $\mu \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{cm}$ ( $\sim 1.25^{\circ}\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{cm}$ ) and at a rate of $1^{\circ} / \mathrm{min}$ . A laboratory-purified lot of $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_4$ used as a spectrographic standard was also examined under identical conditions.

No evidence of a thermal phenomenon could be found at or near $841^{\circ}\mathbf{C}$ on repeated thermal cycling. However, a single endothermic peak was found at $958^{\circ}\mathbf{C}$ on heating the Sr-1909 batch 4 material during the first cycle only. The spectroscopic standard material showed no thermal effect below the melting point. Melting temperatures for the two materials were $1010^{\circ}\mathbf{C}$ and $1020^{\circ}\mathbf{C}$ respectively (literature value $1036^{\circ}\mathbf{C}^{4,5}$ ), and melting occurred over a wide temperature range, indicating lack of purity.

Khripin and co-workers noted that the oxygen content in their system was between 1 and 3 wt%. The two specimens of $\mathrm{UF_4}$ we have examined contained 0.18 and 0.078 wt% oxygen, respectively representing 1.7 and 0.7 mole $\% \mathrm{UO_2}$ .

The present evidence seems to indicate that no polymorphic crystal transition exists in pure $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_4$ at or near $841^{\circ}\mathbf{C}$ , at least within the sensitivity limits of the present measurements, and that the observed effects are most probably due to oxygen species undergoing chemical reaction such as

$$
2 \mathrm {U O F} _ {2} \rightarrow \mathrm {U O} _ {2} + \mathrm {U F} _ {4},
$$

for example, followed by solution of the $\mathrm{UO_2}$ to form a binary system melting at lower temperatures.

# 13.6 ESTIMATION OF ACTIVITY COEFFICIENTS IN LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ MELTS

C. F. Baes, Jr.

Shaffer et al. have collected a considerable amount of data on the following reductive extraction equilibria over a wide range of compositions in LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ mixtures:

$$
\operatorname {L i F} (\mathrm {F}) + \frac {1}{4} \operatorname {T h} ^ {0} (\mathrm {B i}) \rightleftharpoons \frac {1}{4} \operatorname {T h F} _ {4} (\mathrm {F}) + \operatorname {L i} ^ {0} (\mathrm {B i})
$$

$$
\mathrm {C e F} _ {3} (\mathrm {F}) + 3 / 4 \mathrm {T h} ^ {0} (\mathrm {B i}) \rightleftharpoons 3 / 4 \mathrm {T h F} _ {4} (\mathrm {F}) + \mathrm {C e} ^ {0} (\mathrm {B i})
$$

(wherein F and Bi denote, respectively, the molten fluoride and the bismuth phase). Making the reasonable assumption that activity coefficients in the dilute bismuth phase are unity, the equilibrium constants for these reactions may be written

$$
K _ {1} = \left(\frac {D _ {\mathrm {L i}}}{D _ {\mathrm {T h}} ^ {1 / 4}}\right) \left(\frac {f _ {\mathrm {T h F} _ {4}} ^ {1 / 4}}{f _ {\mathrm {L i F}}}\right) \tag {1}
$$

$$
K _ {2} = \left(\frac {D _ {\mathrm {C e}}}{D _ {\mathrm {T h}} ^ {3 / 4}}\right) \left(\frac {f _ {\mathrm {T h F} _ {4}} ^ {3 / 4}}{f _ {\mathrm {C e F} _ {3}}}\right) \tag {2}
$$

(wherein $D_{\mathbf{M}}$ is the observed ratio of mole fraction of $\mathbf{M}^0$ in the bismuth phase to $\mathbf{MF}_z$ in the fluoride phase and $f_{\mathbf{MF}_z}$ is the activity coefficient in the fluoride phase). Barton et al.2 have measured the solubility of $\mathrm{CeF_3}$ also as a function of composition in LiF-BeF2-ThF4 melts. These results give directly the variation of $f_{\mathrm{CeF_3}}$ :

$$
K _ {3} = X _ {\mathrm {C e F} _ {3}} f _ {\mathrm {C e F} _ {3}}. \tag {3}
$$

This, combined with the observed values of $D_{\mathrm{Ce}} / D_{\mathrm{Th}}^{3 / 4}$ should yield the variation with composition of $f_{\mathrm{ThF_4}}$ and this in turn, combined with $D_{\mathrm{Li}} / D_{\mathrm{Th}}^{1 / 4}$ , should give the variation of $f_{\mathrm{LiF}}$ . In short, these data can yield the activity coefficients of all the components over the composition range involved if a suitable means can be found to correlate them.

Such a correlation of activity coefficients is being attempted in $\mathbf{LiF - BeF}_2\mathbf{-MF}_y\mathbf{-NF}_z$ mixtures by an extension of a polymer model previously applied with some success to binary $\mathbf{LiF - BeF}_2$ mixtures.3 As

previously, it is assumed that $\mathbf{Be}^{2+}$ is coordinated by four fluoride ions to form $\mathrm{BeF}_4^{2-}$ tetrahedra and that by the sharing of corners and edges of such tetrahedra (i.e., by the formation of single and double fluoride bridges between $\mathbf{Be}^{2+}$ ions) all the possible polymeric species $\mathrm{Be}_a\mathrm{F}_b^{(b-2a)-}$ are formed in the fluoride mixtures. The relative stability of each of the numerous such species is defined by the equilibrium

$$
a \operatorname {B e F} _ {4} ^ {2 -} \rightleftharpoons \operatorname {B e} _ {a} \operatorname {F} _ {b} ^ {(b - 2 a) -} + (4 a - b) \mathrm {F} ^ {-}
$$

and the corresponding equilibrium constant

$$
\begin{array}{l} K _ {a, b} = \frac {\left(\operatorname {B e} _ {a} \mathrm {F} _ {b} ^ {(b - 2 a) -}\right) \left(\mathrm {F} ^ {-}\right) ^ {(4 a - b)}}{\left(\operatorname {B e F} _ {4} ^ {2 -}\right) ^ {a}} \\ = \exp [ (4 a - b) \alpha_ {2} ], \tag {4} \\ \end{array}
$$

wherein the parentheses denote activities and $\alpha_{2}$ is an adjustable parameter.

It is assumed that the third and the fourth component $\mathbf{MF}_y$ and $\mathbf{NF}_z$ ) form one or several successive mononuclear species, for example, in the case of $\mathbf{MF}_y$

$$
\mathbf {M F} _ {c} ^ {(c - y) -}, \quad \mathbf {M F} _ {c + 1} ^ {(c + 1 - y) -}, \dots
$$

The relative stability of each complex $\mathbf{MF}_b$ is expressed in terms of a stepwise equilibrium constant

$$
\begin{array}{l} K _ {n} = \frac {\left(\mathrm {M F} _ {c + n} ^ {(c + n - y) -}\right)}{\left(\mathrm {M F} _ {c + n - 1} ^ {(c + n - 1 - y) -}\right) \left(\mathrm {F} ^ {-}\right)} \\ = \exp [ \alpha_ {3} - (n - 1) \gamma_ {3} ]. \quad (5): \\ \end{array}
$$

Here the $n$ th stepwise constant is evaluated in terms of a free energy parameter $\alpha_{3}$ analogous to $\alpha_{2}$ for the $\mathrm{Be}_a\mathrm{F}_b$ species. The adjustable parameter $\gamma_{3}$ is included to provide for the expected decrease in the magnitude of successive stepwise constants as the number of bound fluoride ions increases. The overall stability constant for the formation of a particular species $\mathrm{MF}_b^{(b - y)}-$ from the initial species $\mathrm{MF}_c^{(c - y)}-$ and fluoride ion is

$$
\left(\mathrm {M F} _ {b} ^ {(b - y) -}\right)
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left(\mathrm {M F} _ {c} ^ {(c - y) -}\right) \left(\mathrm {F} ^ {-}\right) ^ {b - c} \\ = \exp \left[ (b - c) \alpha_ {3} - \frac {(b - c) (b - c - 1)}{2} \gamma_ {3} \right]. \tag {6} \\ \end{array}
$$

A similar expression may be written for the formation of $\mathbf{NF}_b$ complexes from an initial species in terms of the adjustable parameters $\alpha_4$ and $\gamma_4$ .

As previously, the entropy of mixing of all the species is expressed in terms of the volume fractions $(\phi_{i})$ of each species:

$$
\Delta S _ {m} = - R \sum n _ {i} \ln \phi_ {i}; \tag {7}
$$

$\phi_{i}$ is approximated as the fraction of the total fluoride in the system which is present in species $i$ . The heat of mixing is of the form

$$
\Delta H _ {m} = R T \sum \beta_ {i j} t _ {i} n _ {i} \left(t _ {j} \phi_ {j} / b _ {j}\right), \tag {8}
$$

where the summation is over all the pairwise interactions of the four different kinds of species (1) $\mathbf{F}^{-}$ , (2) $\mathrm{Be}_a\mathrm{F}_b^{(b - 2a) - }$ , (3) $\mathrm{MF}_b^{(b - y) - }$ , (4) $\mathrm{NF}_b^{(b - z) - }$ . The coefficients $t_i$ and $t_j$ are the numbers of nonbridging fluorides on each kind of species:

$$
t _ {1} = 1, t _ {2} = 2 b - 4 a, t _ {3} = b _ {3}, t _ {4} = b _ {4}.
$$

The product $t_i n_i(t_j \phi_j / b_j)$ is intended to be proportional to the number of contacts between nonbridging fluorides of species $i$ and $j$ . The six coefficients $\beta_{12}, \beta_{13}, \beta_{14}, \beta_{23}, \beta_{24}, \beta_{34}$ , reflect the heat effect of such contacts.

Proceeding as previously, the activity of each species is obtained by the partial differentiation

$$
\ln (i) = \frac {\partial \left(\Delta H _ {m} / R T - \Delta S _ {m} / R\right)}{\partial n _ {1}}. \tag {9}
$$

Substitution of the resulting expressions for each activity into Eqs. (4) and (6) yields expressions for the volume fractions of all species in terms of the adjustable $\alpha, \beta,$ and $\gamma$ parameters and the volume fractions of free fluoride, the monomer $\mathrm{BeF}_4^{2-}$ , and the lowest fluoride complexes of $\mathbf{M}^{\gamma+}$ and $\mathbf{N}^{\gamma+}$ . These four volume fractions - $\phi_{\mathbf{F}}$ , $\phi_{\mathrm{BeF}_4}$ , $\phi_{\mathrm{MF}_c}$ , and $\phi_{\mathrm{MF}_d}$ - are determined by an iterative computation involving material balance conditions which are specified by the composition of the mixtures.

With the four volume fractions determined, the activities of the four components in a given mixture are

obtained from the proportionalities

$$
\begin{array}{l} (\operatorname {L i F}) \propto (\operatorname {F} ^ {-}) \\ \left(\mathrm {B e F} _ {2}\right) \propto \left(\mathrm {B e F} _ {4} ^ {2 -}\right) / \left(\mathrm {F} ^ {-}\right) ^ {2} \\ \left(\mathrm {M F} _ {y}\right) \propto \left(\mathrm {M F} _ {c} ^ {(c - y) -}\right) / \left(\mathrm {F} ^ {-}\right) ^ {c - y} \\ \left(\mathrm {N F} _ {z}\right) \propto \left(\mathrm {N F} _ {d} ^ {(d - z) -}\right) / \left(\mathrm {F} ^ {-}\right) ^ {d - z} \\ \end{array}
$$

wherein the activities of $\mathbf{F}^{-}$ , $\mathrm{BeF}_4^{2-}$ , $\mathrm{MF}_c^{(c-y)}$ , and $\mathrm{NF}_d^{(d-z)}$ are obtained by appropriate partial differentiation of $\Delta H_m$ and $\Delta S_m$ [Eq. (9)]. The proportionality constants will be chosen so that the activities of LiF and $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ are unity in 2LiF- $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ and so that the activity coefficients of $\mathrm{MF}_y$ and $\mathrm{NF}_z$ approach unity as $X_{\mathrm{MF}_y}$ and $X_{\mathrm{NF}_z}$ approach zero in this solvent.

In applying this model to the reductive extraction and solubility results $^{1,2}$ in LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ -CeF $_3$ melts, it was found that the data could not be accounted for by the assumption of only one thorium and one cerium species in solution. An acceptable fit was obtained, however, by assuming the following scheme of species (Table 13.4):

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {T h F} _ {4}, \mathrm {T h F} _ {5} ^ {-}, \mathrm {T h F} _ {6} ^ {2 -} \\ \mathrm {C e F} _ {2} ^ {+}, \mathrm {C e F} _ {3}, \mathrm {C e F} _ {4} ^ {-} \\ \end{array}
$$

Here it was necessary to adjust only the $\alpha_{3},\alpha_{4}$ ,and $\beta_{3,4}$ parameters of the model; $\alpha_{2}$ and $\beta_{12}$ were known already from the fit of the model to the binary LiF-BeF $^2$ mixture. The other $\beta$ parameters were set somewhat arbitrarily at the values given in Table 13.4. Thus the first fit was obtained by using three adjustable parameters of the model, along with the three adjustable equilibrium constant values. The standard error of fit was 1.32 times the estimated random error of the data. No significant improvement was obtained by assuming $\gamma_3 = \gamma_4$ and adjusting this additional parameter. A slightly improved fit $(\sigma = 1.24)$ was obtained with the scheme

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {T h F} ^ {3 +}, \mathrm {T h F} _ {2} ^ {2 +}, \dots , \mathrm {T h F} _ {7} ^ {3 -} \\ \mathrm {C e F} ^ {2 + 1}, \mathrm {C e F} _ {2} ^ {+}, \dots , \mathrm {C e F} _ {6} ^ {3 -} \\ \end{array}
$$

and an adjustable $\gamma (= \gamma_3 = \gamma_4)$ parameter. This scheme is essentially the same as the previous one, since the principal species over the composition range involved are the same, but it is preferred as being less arbitrary.

The small number of parameters of the model which require adjustment in order to fit the data suggests that the model is a reasonable representation of the nature of molten $\mathbf{LiF - BeF}_2\cdot \mathbf{ThF}_4$ mixtures and should provide

Table 13.4. Polymer Model for LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ -CeF $_3$ Melts at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$   

<table><tr><td></td><td>Scheme I</td><td>Scheme II</td></tr><tr><td colspan="3">Species assumeda</td></tr><tr><td>BeaFb(b-2a)-plus:</td><td>ThF4, ThF5-, ThF6-2-, CeF2+, CeF3, CeF4-</td><td>ThF3+, ThF22+, ..., ThF43-, CeF2+, CeF2+, ..., CeF63-</td></tr><tr><td colspan="3">Parameters adjustedb</td></tr><tr><td>α3</td><td>2.24</td><td>3.75</td></tr><tr><td>α4</td><td>1.67</td><td>4.42</td></tr><tr><td>γ3, γ4</td><td>(0)</td><td>1.81</td></tr><tr><td>β34</td><td>-0.421</td><td>-0.438</td></tr><tr><td>K1</td><td>0.00316</td><td>0.00328</td></tr><tr><td>K2</td><td>1.60</td><td>1.67</td></tr><tr><td>K3</td><td>0.00583</td><td>0.00589</td></tr><tr><td colspan="3">Parameters not adjusted</td></tr><tr><td>α2c</td><td></td><td>-2.32</td></tr><tr><td>β12c</td><td></td><td>0.43</td></tr><tr><td>β13, β14</td><td></td><td>0.43</td></tr><tr><td>β23, β24</td><td></td><td>0</td></tr><tr><td>Agreement factord</td><td>1.32</td><td>1.24</td></tr></table>

aThe species $\mathbf{Be}_a\mathbf{F}_b^{(b - 2a) - }$ include all such polymeric anions which can be formed by sharing of corners and edges of $\mathbf{BeF_4}$ tetrahedra.   
$b$ The subscripts 1-4 of $a, \beta,$ and $\gamma$ refer, respectively, to the four kinds of species (1) $\mathbf{F}^{-}$ , (2) $\mathrm{Be}_a\mathrm{F}_b^{(b - 2a)^{-}}$ , (3) $\mathrm{ThF}_b^{(b - 4)^{-}}$ , and (4) $\mathrm{CeF}_b^{(b - 3)^{-}}$ . The three $K$ values are defined in Eqs. 1-3.   
$\alpha_{2}$ and $\beta_{2}$ were determined by a previous fit of the model to binary LiF-BeF $_2$ mixtures (ref. 3).

$$
\left[ \sum \left(\frac {X _ {\mathrm {o}} - X _ {\mathrm {c}}}{\sigma_ {x}}\right) ^ {2} / \left(N _ {\mathrm {o}} - N _ {\mathrm {p}}\right) \right] ^ {1 / 2};
$$

$X_{\mathbf{o}}$ is a value of $D_{\mathrm{Li}} / D_{\mathrm{Th}}^{1 / 4}$ or $D_{\mathrm{Ce}} / D_{\mathrm{Th}}^{3 / 4}$ determined by Shaffer et al. (ref. 1), or it is a $\mathrm{CeF_3}$ solubility measured by Barton et al. (ref. 2); $X_{\mathbf{c}}$ is the corresponding calculated quantity; $\sigma_{x}$ is the estimated experimental uncertainty in $X_{\mathbf{o}};N_{\mathbf{o}}$ is the number of observations; $N_{\mathfrak{p}}$ is the number of parameters adjusted.

a good representation of the activity coefficients of the components. Accordingly, activity coefficient curves have been generated (Fig. 13.4) using the parameters of scheme II, Table 13.4. All curves are plotted vs $X_{\mathrm{LiF}}$ which - except in the case of $f_{\mathbf{CeF}_3}$ - seems the most important composition variable determining activity coefficients in these mixtures. All curves are adjusted so that $a_{\mathrm{LiF}}, a_{\mathrm{BeF}_2}, f_{\mathrm{ThF}_4}$ , and $f_{\mathrm{CeF}_3}$ are unity in $\mathbf{Li}_2\mathbf{BcF}_4$ .

Since the $\mathrm{CeF}_3$ curves depend on the relatively more accurate solubility measurements, they should be the most accurate, with an estimated uncertainty not exceeding $10\%$ at compositions farthest from the reference composition. The LiF curves depend partly,

and the $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ curves depend almost entirely, on the less accurate reductive extraction measurement<sup>1</sup> and have uncertainties which may reach $30\%$ at the extremes of the composition range. The $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ curves and LiF curves depend in part on accurate emf data for the binary system<sup>3,4</sup> and should generally be of intermediate accuracy. As more data become available from current emf and other studies, it should be possible with the aid of this model to generate activity coefficient curves for other solutes in these LiF- $\mathrm{BeF_2 - ThF_4}$ melts. This should greatly enhance our ability to predict the effect of melt composition on many important equilibria in MSBR fluoride melts.

![](images/1c91ae431797041ba4e744c012e0767f62dcf0b8b730a1f4a9a464da4685f657.jpg)  
Fig. 13.4. Activity Coefficients of Components in LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ Mixtures Containing a Low Concentration of CeF $_3$ at $600^{\circ}$ C. Curves are based on parameters in scheme II, Table 13.4. Standard states are defined such that $X_{\mathrm{LiF}} \cdot f_{\mathrm{LiF}}, X_{\mathrm{BeF}_2} \cdot f_{\mathrm{BeF}_2}, f_{\mathrm{ThF}_4}$ , and $f_{\mathrm{CeF}_3}$ are all unity in 2LiF-BeF $_2$ (66.7-33.3 mole %).

# 13.7 ESTIMATION OF ACTIVITY COEFFICIENTS OF ALKALINE EARTH'S IN MOLTEN BISMUTH AND AS CHLORIDES OR FLUORIDES

J.Braunstein

A promising modification of the reductive extraction process for removal of rare-earth fission products from molten-salt breeder reactor fuels employs an acceptor salt such as molten lithium chloride contacting the bismuth phase. Thermodynamic data suggest that the fission product alkali metals, Rb and Cs, probably will concentrate in the chloride phase. Ba and Sr should have a smaller tendency to transfer to the chloride, but in trying to calculate distribution ratios of Cs, Rb, Ba, and Sr in the process, activity coefficients are needed for the fluoride salts, chloride salts, and the metals in the bismuth phase. Direct experimental measurements of the needed thermodynamic quantities are not yet available. However, recent advances in molten-salt solution theory have been made which provide a useful guide to the estimation of activity coefficients from data in related systems. Estimates are given in Table 13.5 of the activity coefficient, $\gamma$ , of $\mathrm{SrCl}_2$ and $\mathrm{BaCl}_2$ as dilute solutions in molten LiCl, of $\mathrm{SrF}_2$ and $\mathrm{BaF}_2$ as dilute solutions in LiF, and of Sr and Ba as dilute solutions in molten bismuth. Also listed are the excess chemical potentials, $\mu^{\mathrm{E}} = RT\ln \gamma$ , at infinite dilution of the above solutes in LiCl, LiF, or Bi.

Free energy data have been summarized for mixtures of KCl or of NaCl with $\mathrm{CaCl}_2$ , $\mathrm{SrCl}_2$ , or $\mathrm{BaCl}_2$ and of LiCl with $\mathrm{MgCl}_2$ . Conformal ionic solution theory predicts a linear relation between the excess chemical potential at infinite dilution of divalent salt and an interionic distance parameter. This correlation has been applied to the chloride mixtures and permits estimation of $\mu_{\mathrm{SrCl}_2}^{\mathrm{E}}$ and $\mu_{\mathrm{BaCl}_2}^{\mathrm{E}}$ in molten LiCl with a short linear extrapolation.

Table 13.5. Activity Coefficients and Excess Chemical Potentials for Dilute Solutions of Sr or Ba as Chlorides, as Fluorides, or in Molten Bismuth   

<table><tr><td>Solvent</td><td>Solute</td><td>μE(kcal per mole of solute)a</td><td>γb</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="2">LiCl</td><td>SrCl2</td><td>+1</td><td>1.7</td></tr><tr><td>BaCl2</td><td>+2</td><td>3.0</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="2">LiF</td><td>SrF2</td><td>+1</td><td>1.7</td></tr><tr><td>BaF2</td><td>+2</td><td>3.0</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="2">Bi</td><td>Ba</td><td>-52 to -44</td><td>2 × 10-12to 1 × 10-10</td></tr><tr><td>Sr</td><td>-44</td><td>10-10</td></tr></table>

${}^{a}$ Solution at infinite dilution.   
$b650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , for supercooled liquid when below melting point of solute or solvent.

For the fluoride mixtures, it was assumed that activity coefficients of divalent solutes in $\mathbf{LiF - BeF_2}$ mixtures are similar to those in LiF. Further, asymmetry in the free energy of mixing in alkali fluoride—alkaline earth fluoride systems was neglected, and

$$
\left(\mu_ {\text {s o l u t e}} ^ {\mathrm {E}}\right) _ {X _ {\text {s o l u t e}} = 0}
$$

was estimated as

$$
\left(\mu_ {\text {s o l v e n t}} ^ {\mathrm {E}} / X _ {\text {s o l u t e}} ^ {2}\right) _ {X _ {\text {s o l u t e}} = 0},
$$

again using the correlation provided by conformal ionic solution theory.5,6

Free energy data for Sr and Ba in molten bismuth are sparse and are difficult to obtain because of the activity of these alkaline earth metals. Previous estimates of $\gamma_{\mathrm{Ba}}$ range between $10^{-18}$ and $10^{-6}$ . Estimates of the activity coefficients can be obtained from a plot of enthalpies of formation (per gram atom of metal) of the compounds BaBi, $\mathrm{Ba}_3\mathrm{Bi}_2$ , SrBi, $\mathrm{Sr}_3\mathrm{Bi}_2$ , and $\mathrm{Sr}_2\mathrm{Bi}^{9-11}$ vs composition by extrapolation of the slope to infinite dilution. The activity coefficient of Mg in Bi, estimated in the same manner, is within a factor of 2 of the measured activity coefficient.

# 13.8 POTENTIOMETRIC STUDIES IN MOLTEN FLUORIDES

B. F. Hitch C. F. Baes, Jr.

We have continued our studies of the cells

$$
\operatorname {B e} (c) \mid \operatorname {L i F}, \operatorname {B e F} _ {2} \mid \operatorname {R}. \operatorname {E}.
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {P t} (c) \left| \begin{array}{l} \mathrm {U F} _ {3}, \mathrm {U F} _ {4}, \\ \mathrm {L i F}, \mathrm {B e F} _ {2} \end{array} \right| \mathrm {R}. \mathrm {E}. \\ \end{array} \tag {II}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {N i} (c) \left\lfloor \begin{array}{l} \mathrm {N i F} _ {2}, \\ \mathrm {L i F}, \mathrm {B e F} _ {2} \end{array} \right\rfloor \mathrm {R . E .} \end{array} \tag {III}
$$

in which the reference electrode is nickel immersed in molten $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{i}_2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{e}\mathbf{F}_4$ saturated with NiO and BeO:

$$
\mathrm {R . E .} = \left| \begin{array}{l l} \mathrm {N i F} _ {2} (\sim 1 0 ^ {- 4}), & \text {B e O} (c), \\ \mathrm {L i F} (0. 6 7), & \mathrm {N i O} (c) \\ \mathrm {B e F} _ {2} (0. 3 3) & \end{array} \right| \mathrm {N i} (c)
$$

In previous measurements of these cells, this reference electrode, contained in a fritted silica tube, has proven relatively reliable and especially convenient for potentiometric studies of molten fluorides; however, the gradual attack of the silica by the fluoride melts, with the production of gaseous $\mathrm{SiF_4}$ and dissolved oxide, has caused difficulties. In the current measurements, we have investigated the use of sintered BeO as a container material for the reference electrode.

The compartments used, obtained from Brush Berylium Corporation, were $99.8\%$ BeO. They had been closed at the lower end by a disk of the same sintered BeO. In initial tests it was found, fortunately, that the seal between the disk and the end of each tube was not complete, but in fact contained holes or cracks as large as $2\mu$ . The electrical resistance measured with molten salt both inside and outside these compartments was quite low, typically 75 ohms. Yet salt leakage through these compartments appeared to be very small. These compartments have been exposed to $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ melts for periods up to three weeks with no detectable weight loss or visible signs of attack.

Cell I. - Since the BeO compartments seemed practically impervious to salt we were concerned with the possibility of BeO acting as a solid electrolyte. If we assume that the $\mathrm{Be}^{2+}$ ion or the $\mathbf{O}^{2-}$ is carrying the current, the cell reaction for cell I above would be

$$
\mathrm {B e} ^ {0} (c) + \mathrm {N i O} (c) \rightarrow \mathrm {B e O} (c) + \mathrm {N i} ^ {0} (c) \tag {1}
$$

and for either case the cell potential would be independent of melt composition. However, if there is liquid contact and we assume that all the current is carried across the liquid junction by the $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{i}^{+}$ ion we may then write the following cell reaction:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {B e} ^ {0} (c) + \mathrm {N i O} (c) + \mathrm {B e F} _ {2} (d, R) + 2 \mathrm {L i F} (d) \rightarrow \mathrm {N i} ^ {0} (c) \\ + \operatorname {B e O} (c) + \operatorname {B e F} _ {2} (d) + 2 \operatorname {L i F} (d, R); \tag {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

$R$ designates solutes in the reference compartment. Since the reference solution closely approximated the composition of the compound $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ , the standard state we have adopted for $\mathrm{LiF}(d)$ and $\mathrm{BeF}_2(d)$ , then $a_{\mathrm{LiF}(R)}$ and $a_{\mathrm{BeF}_2(R)}$ are unity, and the cell potential is given by

$$
E = E ^ {\circ} - \frac {R T}{2 F} \ln \frac {a _ {\mathrm {B e F} _ {2}}}{a _ {\mathrm {L i F}} ^ {2}}. \tag {3}
$$

To check the effect, or lack of effect, of melt composition on the cell potential we varied the composition of the $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2}$ mixture on the left in cell I. Results are shown in Fig. 13.5, where they are plotted against activity data from previous measurements. As can be seen, the cell potential does vary with melt composition, showing a dependence on $a_{\mathrm{BeF}_2} / a_{\mathrm{LiF}}^2$ which agrees quite well with Eq. (3), represented by the line in Fig. 13.5. This result suggests strongly that the current is being carried from one half-cell to the other by $\mathrm{Li^{+}}$ ion through the molten salt, rather than by mobile $\mathrm{Be}^{2 + }$ or $\mathbf{O}^{2 - }$ ions in the solid BeO.

A series of measurements of cell I containing $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{i}_2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{e}\mathbf{F}_4$ was made over the temperature range 500 to $700^{\circ}C.$ The results differ by about $5\mathrm{mv}$ from those obtained previously with the use of silica compartments. The present results give

$$
E _ {I} ^ {\circ} = 1. 8 9 2 - 0. 0 4 4 (T / 1 0 0 0), \tag {4}
$$

where $T$ is the temperature in degrees Kelvin. This expression for the potential of cell I, when combined with our previous measurements of the $\mathrm{Be}^{2+}/\mathrm{Be}^0$ couple vs the $\mathrm{HF/H}_2$ , $\mathbf{F}^{-}$ electrode, gives the expression in Table 13.6 for the potential of the NiO, $\mathrm{Be}^{2+}/\mathrm{BeO}$ , $\mathbf{Ni}^0$ reference electrode vs the $\mathrm{HF/H}_2$ , $\mathbf{F}^{-}$ electrode.

Table 13.6. Electrode Potentials vs HF/H2, F   

<table><tr><td>1. Be2+ + 2e- → Be(c)</td><td>E° = -2.460 + 0.694(T/1000)</td></tr><tr><td>2. U4+ + e- → U3+</td><td>E° = -1.106 (610°C)</td></tr><tr><td>3. HF + e- → F- + 1/2H2</td><td>E° = 0</td></tr><tr><td>4. Be2+ + NiO(c) + 2e- → BeO(c) + Ni(c)</td><td>E° = 0.568 + 0.650(T/1000)</td></tr><tr><td>5. Ni2+ 2e- → Ni(c)</td><td>E° = 0.343 (605°C)</td></tr></table>

The above measurements were made over a period of three weeks, and a single $\mathbf{Be}^0$ electrode was used. There was no tendency of the cell potential to run down, nor was there any other indication that the $\mathbf{Be}^0$ electrode was being "poisoned" by the reduction of reducible metals on the electrode surface. This is strong evidence that there was no significant leakage of $\mathbf{Ni}^{2+}$ ion from the reference electrode compartment.

![](images/e7e260c8fab11c3f968772ad254cea2f13f53195171c37f51401acdcef202d62.jpg)  
Fig. 13.5. Potential Dependence of Cell I on the Ratio of $a_{\mathrm{BeF}_2} / a_{\mathrm{LiF}}^2$ .

Cell II, the $\mathbf{U}^{4 + } / \mathbf{U}^{3 + }$ Couple. - Studies of cell II are continuing with the reference electrode compartmented in BeO instead of silica. Present measurements give a value of 1.110 v at $610^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $E^{\circ}$ in the Nernst expression

$$
E = E ^ {\infty} - \frac {R T}{F} \ln \left(\frac {X _ {\mathrm {U F} _ {4}}}{X _ {\mathrm {U F} _ {3}}}\right) \tag {5}
$$

for $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ as the solvent. This may be compared with our previous measurements of $E^{\circ}$ , which gave 1.067 v, and the predicted value of 1.139 v. The uncertainties in the present measurements should be no more than $\pm 15$ mv. The potential of the $\mathrm{U}^{4+}/\mathrm{U}^{3+}$ couple vs the $\mathrm{HF/H_2}$ , $\mathbf{F}^{-}$ couple (Table 13.6) was calculated by combining the measured cell potential of cell II and the half-cell potential of the reference electrode. We plan to make further measurements using the BeO compartments in further attempts to resolve the inconsistencies noted above as well as to establish a more accurate standard reference potential for the cell.

Cell III, the $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}/\mathrm{Ni}^{0}$ Couple. - The cell reaction for cell III, above, with $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{BeF}_{4}$ in both half-cells and at low concentrations of $\mathrm{NiF}_{2}$ may be written

$$
\operatorname {B e O} (c) + \operatorname {N i F} _ {2} (d) \rightarrow \operatorname {B e F} _ {2} (d) + \operatorname {N i O} (c). \tag {6}
$$

Then the cell potential should be given by

$$
E = E ^ {\circ} + \frac {R T}{2 F} \ln X _ {\mathrm {N i F} _ {2}}. \tag {7}
$$

Cell potentials with the reference electrode compartmented in silica were determined by titrating weighed amounts of anhydrous $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ into an $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ melt at $605^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . A plot of the results is shown in Fig. 13.6. The measured cell potentials have an estimated uncertainty of $\pm 10\mathrm{mv}$ and differ by some $30\mathrm{mv}$ from those based on the equilibrium measurements of Blood. Measurements have been attempted using BeO compartments for the reference electrode but have not yielded consistent results; further investigations are planned.

![](images/9ebea3a817f7dc3b387310d75f3a72e03846e3bd11820cc4002142656ef58501.jpg)  
Fig. 13.6. Measured Cell Potentials of Cell III.

The measured cell potential in volts at $605^{\circ}\mathbf{C}$ is given by

$$
E = 0. 3 4 3 + 0. 0 8 7 \log X _ {\mathrm {N i F} _ {2} (d)}. \tag {8}
$$

The potential for the $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}/\mathrm{Ni}^{0}$ couple vs the $\mathrm{HF}/\mathrm{H}_{2}$ , $\mathbf{F}^{-}$ electrode (Table 13.6) was calculated by combining the potential of cell III and the reference electrode half-cell potential.

# 13.9 ELECTRICAL CONDUCTIVITIES AND IONIC MOBILITIES IN THE MOLTEN LiF-BeF $_2$ SYSTEM

G. D. Robbins J. Braunstein

In addition to its relevance to the molten-salt reactor program, investigation of electrical transport in molten $\mathsf{LiF - BeF}_2$ mixtures is of considerable theoretical interest. The binary $\mathsf{LiF - BeF}_2$ system is comprised of pure components exhibiting transport properties which differ by over seven orders of magnitude. Both in its transport properties and its thermodynamic properties it exhibits characteristics of network liquids at the $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ end and of a typical ionic liquid at the LiF end. $^{1-3}$

The results of electrical conductivity measurements in $\mathsf{LiF - BeF_2}$ mixtures ranging in composition from 34 to 70 mole $\%$ beryllium fluoride presented here contain data from two previous experiments $^{4,5}$ and afford an analysis of the variation of electrical conductivity with both composition and temperature. Ionic mobilities have been reported previously $^{6}$ at two temperatures and two compositions using transference number $^{6}$ and density $^{7}$ data. The new conductivity data reported here permit an extended evaluation of the composition dependence of internal ionic mobilities.

Cell constants of the four cells employed in these studies ranged from $98.5_{5}$ to $150.4\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ as determined by previously described techniques. Conductances in the molten fluorides were measured with a specially constructed ac bridge which contains a variable resistance and capacitance, in series, in the balancing arm and has the capability of variation of voltage and frequency of the applied sinusoidal potential. Applied potentials were 25 to $50\mathrm{mv}$ peak-to-peak across the cell. For resistances of the order of 100 ohms the bridge inaccuracy is $< \pm 0.1\%$ over the frequency range 0.1 to $30\mathrm{kHz}$ and increases toward $\pm 0.2\%$ as resistances approach 10 or 500 ohms.

As has been previously discussed,4,9 the frequency range over which resistance is independent of frequency (or varies in a regular manner) must be determined for each experimental arrangement and electrolyte solution. This was again demonstrated in the current investigation, in which a frequency range could be found over which the molten $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ used in cell constant determination exhibited no variation of resistance with frequency, within the $\pm 0.2\%$ uncertainty limits of the bridge, for each of the four cells (the ranges were 1 to 20, 1 to 10, 0.5 to 3, and 3 to 30 kHz). With molten fluorides in the cells, two of the mixtures showed a frequency-independent region (3 to $30~\mathrm{kHz}$ and 5 to $50~\mathrm{kHz}$ ), while in the other two,

resistances were approximately linear in $1 / \sqrt{f}$ (2% variation, 0.5 to 5 kHz, in one; 0.5% variation, 1 to 10 kHz, in the other). In these melts measured resistances were extrapolated to infinite frequency vs $1 / \sqrt{f}$ .

Specific conductances are listed in Table 13.7. These were obtained from four sets of experiments in different cells (see Fig. 13.7). The upper temperature limit of approximately $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was imposed by use of molten potassium nitrate as a molten salt bath. In one experiment (cell 2) no bath was present, permitting an upper temperature limit of $647^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to be attained. However, as the data obtained with cell 2 reflect (Fig. 13.7), the absence of a bath around the cell resulted in greater scatter, probably due to temperature variation in the conducting region of the cell.

As can be seen from Fig. 13.7, the specific conductance data vary smoothly as a function of temperature. The curves shown represent computer-fitted least-squares equations that were used to generate the isotherms of specific conductance plotted in Fig. 13.8 as a function of composition. The points shown are calculated values (at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ intervals) for each experimental composition and extend to the next $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ beyond the actual data range. It is believed that the divergence of the data at $X = 0.540$ from a smooth curve at higher temperatures is due to experimental scatter. That the data obtained at $X = 0.500$ with cell 4 form a smooth curve with data from cell 1 at compositions on either side, lends added credence to the results.

$$
\kappa = \left(a _ {1} + a _ {2} X\right) \exp \left[ \frac {- b _ {1} e ^ {b _ {2} X}}{T - \left(c _ {1} + c _ {2} X\right)} \right]
$$

$\pmb{T}$ in degrees Kelvin   

<table><tr><td>Parameter</td><td>Value</td></tr><tr><td>a1</td><td>7.1845</td></tr><tr><td>a2</td><td>7.7458</td></tr><tr><td>b1</td><td>252.57</td></tr><tr><td>b2</td><td>3.2965</td></tr><tr><td>c1</td><td>467.24</td></tr><tr><td>c2</td><td>-333.96</td></tr></table>

Rather than reporting the 49 parameter values required for interpolation employing the individual equations shown in Figs. 13.7 and 13.8, it is more convenient to represent the specific conductance as a function of temperature and composition by a single expression with coefficients derived by least-squares fitting of all the data simultaneously. One such six-parameter relation is

$$
\kappa = \left(a _ {1} + a _ {2} X\right) \exp \left[ \frac {- b _ {1} e ^ {b _ {2} X}}{T - \left(c _ {1} + c _ {2} X\right)} \right]; \tag {1}
$$

$T$ is in degrees Kelvin. Coefficients for Eq. (1) are listed in Table 13.8. The standard error of fit is 0.007.

Based on the assumption of randomness and independence of errors with an estimated uncertainty in $\kappa$ of $0.4\%$ from cell constant determination, $0.2\%$ from resistance measurement, $0.5\%$ from resistance vs $1/\sqrt{f}$ extrapolation, and $0.3\%$ from temperature measure

![](images/afffd29f844fbbbe1c5a049a6ced789ba0bdda896969f72c0b28e6a4aa469c3d.jpg)  
Fig. 13.8. Specific Conductance of LiF-BeF $_2$ Mixtures vs Composition.

ment, the calculated probable error is $0.8\%$ . We believe the uncertainty level of these results to be of the order of $\pm 2\%$ .

Mobilities of lithium ion relative to fluoride (or relative to beryllium ion, since beryllium is immobile relative to fluoride) may be calculated from the relation

$$
b _ {\mathrm {L i}} = \frac {t _ {\mathrm {L i}} \kappa}{F C _ {\mathrm {L i}}} = \frac {\kappa}{F C _ {\mathrm {L i}}},
$$

where $C_{\mathrm{Li}}$ is the concentration of lithium ion in equivalents per cubic centimeter, $F$ is the faraday, and $t_\mathrm{Li}$ is the transference number of lithium ion relative to fluoride, which is unity. The mobilities of lithium relative to fluoride have been calculated at $25^{\circ}$ intervals and are shown in Fig. 13.9. These, extrapolated to $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , are compared in Fig. 13.10 with the mobilities of Li relative to halide in the binary $\mathrm{LiCl - KCl}$ , $\mathrm{LiBr - KBr}$ , and $\mathrm{LiCl - PbCl}_2$ .

to halide in the KCl-CaCl $_2$ and KCl-MgCl $_2$ systems. In the LiF-BeF $_2$ system, values of $b_{\mathrm{Li}}$ are smaller than those in the other binary system of halides, as might be expected with increased network character of the liquid.

![](images/cf7c77fabd939f2950658f829711ee943c4a32bd4573d18b57bd9279a01af1be.jpg)  
Fig. 13.9. Internal Mobility (Lithium Relative to Fluoride) as a Function of Composition in the System LiF-BeF $_2$ .

![](images/a1165412c2ab841e609800c88901bf52bad8cb9917a65e48881482c1dfc8f323.jpg)  
Fig. 13.10. Internal Mobilities (Lithium or Potassium Relative to the Anion) vs Mole Fraction of the Second Component.

# 13.10 ELECTRICAL CONDUCTIVITIES OF PROPOSED MSBR FUEL COMPOSITIONS IN THE LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ SYSTEM

G. D. Robbins A. S. Gallanter

The absence of reliable theories for predicting electrical transport in molten salts renders a priori estimation of electrical conductance in ternary mixtures extremely uncertain. Hence, specific conductances and their temperature dependences from near the liquidus to $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ have been measured for four proposed MSBR fuel compositions. Two additional $\mathsf{LiF - BeF}_2\cdot \mathsf{ThF}_4$ compositions having equal molar ratios to individual proposed fuel compositions were also investigated to determine the effect of additions of each component.

Starting materials were of compositions (in mole percent $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ThF_4}$ 68-20-12, 63-25-12, and 72-21-7. These had been previously treated with an $\mathbf{H}_2 / \mathbf{HF}$ mixture followed by reduction with pure hydrogen. A liquid aliquot of each starting material was delivered into a nickel vessel under argon, frozen, and opened in a dry box; the entire sample was ground to 30 mesh particle size and mixed on a rotating tumbler for at least $3\mathrm{hr}$ to ensure homogeneity. Two additional compositions (60.5-28-11.5 and 71-18.13-10.87) were obtained by addition of either clear pieces of lithium fluoride, which had been fused under a hydrogen-argon mixture and slowly crystallized, or sublimed beryllium fluoride glass. A sixth composition (73-16-11) was prepared from the component starting materials, the $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ having been treated with $\mathbf{H}_2 / \mathbf{HF}$ followed by $\mathbf{H}_2$

The silica conductance cells and measuring bridge are described elsewhere. Cell constants ranged in value from 93.09 to $156.12\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ and were independent of frequency $(\pm 0.1\%)$ from 10 to $50\mathrm{kHz}$ as determined in molten potassium nitrate. With molten fluorides in the cells, some showed no frequency dependence of the resistance, while the resistances of others varied as $1 / \sqrt{f}$ and were extrapolated to infinite frequency. Assigned uncertainties in the determination of specific conductance are $0.4\%$ from cell constant determination,

0.2% from resistance measurement, 0.3% from temperature measurement, 0.5% from $1 / \sqrt{f}$ extrapolation, and 1.5% from temperature gradients resulting from the absence of a molten nitrate bath due to the high temperatures. Specific conductance data for six $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2}\cdot \mathrm{ThF_4}$ mixtures are given in Table 13.9. The temperature variation of specific conductance is well represented by linear equations, as can be seen from Fig. 13.11. The computer-fitted least-squares equations represented in Fig. 13.11 and the standard error of fit, $\sigma$ , for each composition are listed in Table 13.10.

Three pairs of curves have a constant ratio of two of the components. The effect of variation of the molar concentration of the third component is shown in Table 13.11. At the compositions noted, addition of LiF at constant molar ratio of $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ to $\mathrm{ThF}_4$ results in an increase in the specific conductance. Addition of either $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ or $\mathrm{ThF}_4$ at constant molar ratio of the remaining two components lowers the specific conductance. These measurements supersede estimates of conductance for four proposed MSBR fuel compositions.

# 13.11 DETERMINATION OF LIQUIDUS TEMPERATURES IN THE LiF-BeF $_2$ SYSTEM FROM EMF MEASUREMENTS OF TRANSFERENCE CELLS

K.A.Romberger J.Braunstein

Emf measurements of cells with transference provide a method by which, in favorable cases, precise determinations can be made of liquidus or solid saturation temperatures in multicomponent systems. The technique, to be useful, requires that the emf be stable, reproducible, and responsive to the change of activity in the melt accompanying a phase change occurring in one of the half-cells. The phase change may be engendered either by changing the temperature at constant total composition or by changing the composition at constant temperature. In either case, plots of emf vs composition or emf vs temperature show discontinuities of slope at the liquidus composition or temperature.

Our studies of the emf's of the cell with transference

$$
\mathrm {B e} \left| \begin{array}{l} \mathrm {L i F} \\ \mathrm {B e F} _ {2} \\ \mathrm {I} \end{array} \right| \left| \begin{array}{l} \mathrm {L i F} \\ \mathrm {B e F} _ {2} \\ \mathrm {I I} \end{array} \right| \mathrm {B e} \tag {1}
$$

![](images/d9546361b5b99b3e3f2f1c7e0bdbe2bfea6fa3dfe4658af015e7e902f77061a7.jpg)  
Fig. 13.12. Data Plots Illustrating the Two Graphical Methods for the Determination of Liquidus Temperature. (a) Plot of EMF vs salt composition. Temperature constant at $607.5 \pm 0.1^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . (b) Plot of EMF vs temperature. Total bulk salt composition 0.2997 mole fraction $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ .

indicated that the system $\mathbf{LiF - BeF}_2$ was particularly amenable to precise phase boundary determinations with the cell (1). Measurements were made in which the reference solution (I) remained homogeneous while the melt in the bulk compartment (II) was transformed from a homogeneous solution to one containing the equilibrium saturating solid, either by additions of one component isothermally, or by lowering the temperature of the entire cell. The measurements have yielded liquidus temperatures in the $\mathbf{LiF - BeF}_2$ system for compositions between 0.12 and 0.58 mole fraction $\mathbf{BeF}_2$ . Experimental details of the emf measurements are given in ref. 1.

Liquidus temperatures and compositions were derived from plots of emf vs the composition of compartment II at constant temperature, as in Fig. 13.12a, and from plots of emf vs temperature at a fixed overall composition of compartment II, as in Fig. 13.12b. These plots are representative of the sensitivity of the method, namely, about 0.0002 to 0.0003 mole fraction $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ in composition and $\pm 0.2^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in temperature.

The liquidus-temperature-composition results are shown in Table 13.12. These data were obtained from two independent experiments, which overlap along the LiF liquidus for approximately a $50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ temperature interval. The internal consistency of each set of points is within $\pm 0.0002$ mole fraction $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ , but there appears to be an offset of approximately 0.0015 mole fraction $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ between the two sets. This probably results in part from an uncertainty of $\pm 0.001$ mole fraction $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ in the correction of the composition, in one of the experiments, for material withdrawn from

the bulk compartment into the small reference compartment.

An interesting aspect of the $\mathbf{LiF - BeF_2}$ system has been whether the solid compound $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ melts congruently, as do the other alkali fluoroberyllates, or incongruently. Three different solid phases have been shown to exist in equilibrium with the liquid phase of $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2}$ : LiF, $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ , and $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ . If, when the total bulk composition is 0.3333 mole fraction $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ , the solid in equilibrium with the liquid is $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF_4}$ , then the compound melts congruently, and, in addition, an $\mathrm{LiF - Li_2BeF_4}$ eutectic exists (although it might coincide with the melting point). If the equilibrium solid at 0.3333 mole fraction $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ is LiF, then melting of $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF_4}$ is incongruent, and there is a peritectic. The latest résumé of this system by Thoma et al. interpreted the available data to suggest that $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF_4}$ melted incongruently. We believe the precision and accuracy of the emf method, however, to be capable of resolving the question virtually unequivocally, and the new results indicate that $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF_4}$ melts congruently. The evidence for this conclusion is presented below.

When the emf is measured of a cell in which a homogeneous solution is cooled until precipitation occurs, the observed emf indicates the composition of the solution phase regardless of whether solid is present or not. The direction of the emf change after the onset of precipitation is determined by whether the composition of $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ in the melt increases or decreases.

LiF</td><td>0.3056</td><td>516.7</td></tr><tr><td>0.1188</td><td>784.7</td><td>0.3061</td><td>518.2</td></tr><tr><td>0.1215</td><td>782.9</td><td>0.3119</td><td>502.8</td></tr><tr><td>0.1371</td><td>770.6</td><td>0.3141</td><td>495.9</td></tr><tr><td>0.1404</td><td>767.9</td><td>0.3179</td><td>486.3</td></tr><tr><td>0.1574</td><td>753.2</td><td>0.3189</td><td>483.7</td></tr><tr><td>0.1625</td><td>747.9</td><td>0.3208</td><td>480.5</td></tr><tr><td>0.1853</td><td>723.9</td><td>0.3216</td><td>477.2</td></tr><tr><td>0.1894</td><td>718.0</td><td>0.3242</td><td>470.1</td></tr><tr><td>0.2053</td><td>699.5</td><td>0.3261</td><td>463.1</td></tr><tr><td>0.2066</td><td>698.0</td><td>0.3285</td><td>458.2</td></tr></table>

Comparing a series of mixtures whose initial compositions cross the composition of the compound, as indicated schematically in Fig. 13.13, the cell emf increases during precipitation for initial compositions on one side of the compound composition, and decreases on the other side. For an incongruity melting compound the emf's will always change in the same direction. Thus a compound, peritectic, or eutectic point can be identified. It should be noted that this argument depends only on the response of the Be electrodes to $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ concentrations and therefore yields the topology of the liquidus line independent of possible errors in values of temperature or composition.

For compositions within $\pm 0.005$ mole fraction of the $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{i}_2\mathbf{B}\mathbf{e}\mathbf{F}_4$ composition, the liquidus curve was found to be very flat; that is, the melting temperature was quite insensitive to composition change. Hence a large fraction of the total salt present had to precipitate or dissolve to lower or raise the temperature an experi

mentally meaningful amount, say $0.1^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Because of this heat load, it was not feasible to increment the equilibrium temperature when a large fraction of solid was present.

Hence, rather than trying to maintain temperature equilibrium, we cooled the solution slowly at a nearly constant rate. The emf was recorded as a function of time both before and after precipitation occurred. Figure 13.14 shows a plot of emf vs time where all the initial emf's have been shifted to a common zero value in order to emphasize the changes. The fingered pattern shows that the solution composition moved first toward higher and then toward lower $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ compositions as the initial overall $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ concentration was lowered. LiF additions 96 and 97 showed very little emf change while precipitation was occurring. The calculated compositions of these solutions were 0.3337 and 0.3329 mole fraction $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ , compared with the theoretical value of 0.33333 for $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ . X-ray data show that
